title: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum
summary: The checked-in SSecr XML is a pinned University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative encoding dated 1997 and keyed from Robert Steele's 1898 EETS Extra Series 74 edition. It preserves Steele's short Note and three distinct Middle English version streams while omitting other front matter and Henderson's glossary. Steele prints version one from British Library Royal MS 18 A VII; version two chiefly from Lambeth MS 501, supplying its mutilated f. 18 from Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 685; and James Yonge's 1422 version chiefly from Bodleian MS Rawl. B. 490, supplying a Rawlinson lacuna from Lambeth MS 633. Laing reconstructs a proximate common exemplar behind Lambeth 501 and Laud 685, so neither is modeled as copied from the other. The second version's direct source remains disputed between Steele's Latin-vulgate claim and Manzalaoui's French-F3 claim. The XML's L-marked second milestone sequence likely maps Lambeth 633 but remains unconfirmed. A complete public scan of the 1898 edition and compact supporting sources are cached. No complete free public facsimile was verified for any of the five physical manuscripts: Royal, Lambeth 501, Laud 685, and Rawlinson have none in the exact public interfaces checked; Lambeth 633 exposes only two selected LUNA images, of which f. 84r contains the work's ending and f. 85v contains only later pen trials. Physical, microfilm, and mediated-reproduction routes are recorded without treating them as public facsimiles.
review_notes: Cataloging subject is the exact three-version electronic publication set, not one undifferentiated abstract Secretum secretorum.
Textual, physical, editorial, digital, and reproduction layers are kept separate; rights in one layer are not transferred to another.
The source cache was built in the canonical repository and hard-linked into the dedicated research worktree; final inode and checksum checks are required before integration.
Negative facsimile findings are dated interface searches, not claims that no private, onsite, or future photography exists.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for SSecr
entities.description: The publication-bound repository file at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/SSecr.xml. It contains an outer Steele Note and three grouped TEXT children corresponding to the Royal, Lambeth, and Yonge versions.
notes: The file is 628,610 bytes and carries IDNO SSecr.
The CME submodule's Unlicense is software/repository evidence and is not treated as a blanket rights statement for encoded texts, editions, manuscript images, or provider metadata.

entities.label: University of Michigan HTI electronic edition of SSecr
entities.description: A 1997 electronic text keyed from Steele's 1898 edition, later completely proofed and corrected by Jean Borger and Kenneth Church and reviewed by Catherine E. Paul. Its editorial declaration includes print notes but excludes textual glosses, regularizes soft-hyphen line breaks, retains the second version's book divisions and the third version's dual milestone sets, and omits other front matter and the glossary.
notes: The pinned file's legacy availability paragraph permits personal, research, and teaching copies with its statement and links, while forbidding redistribution by commercial or non-profit content providers.

The current U-M bulk-download description says CME raw files are available without restrictions on use or reuse. Because the item-specific embedded statement is stricter, both statements are retained instead of silently replacing one with the other.

entities.label: Steele, Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum (1898)
entities.description: The immediate print source of the U-M encoding: Robert Steele's EETS edition of three Middle English prose versions, with T. Henderson's glossary. Steele printed the Royal, Lambeth, and Yonge versions on pp. 1–248, supplied manuscript lacunae from collateral witnesses, and printed the glossary on pp. 249–293.

bibliographic.citation: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum, ed. Robert Steele, with a glossary by T. Henderson, vol. I, EETS Extra Series 74 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1898).
bibliographic.title: Three Prose Versions of the Secreta Secretorum
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 74

notes: The volume calls itself vol. I. No second published volume was verified.
The introduction and notes announced on the title page were postponed; the short Note explains the accelerated publication of Yonge's version.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Steele's 1898 edition
entities.description: A complete University of Toronto/Pratt Library scan retaining covers, title matter, Steele's Note, all three edited texts, glossary, and bound volume matter. The PDF, full OCR, IA metadata, and scan-data package are cached.
entities.reproduction_format: 312-page PDF scan with OCR and scan metadata

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 18 A VII
entities.description: A 30-folio parchment codex written in one neat mid-fifteenth-century hand. The shortened-French-derived Secrete of Secretes occupies f. 1r through f. 26r; a separate pseudo-Bernard epistle begins f. 26v.

holding.notes: Formerly belonged to John, Lord Lumley; the target is item 1.

notes: Both codex items are in the same neat hand.

entities.label: Reconstructed proximate common exemplar of Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685
entities.description: The common textual exemplar X reconstructed by Margaret Laing from shared omissions, errors, rare forms at the same running-text positions, and synchronized language changes in Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685. It is a transmission hypothesis, not the authorial original or an extant cataloged manuscript.
notes: Laing argues that disjunctive relict forms make direct copying of either extant manuscript from the other extremely unlikely.
No shelfmark, holding institution, date, or public reproduction is known for X.

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 501
entities.description: The principal witness for Steele's Governance of Lordschipes text. James describes a late-fifteenth-century parchment codex of one flyleaf plus 44 folios in a clear hand; f. 18 is mutilated, and the Secretum ends f. 42r. Steele supplied the lost f. 18 passage from Laud Misc. 685.

notes: 32 lines to a page; modern morocco binding; f. 18 mutilated.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Laud Misc. 685
entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment codex whose first item is the same Middle English Secretum version as Lambeth 501, occupying ff. 1r–57v according to Laing. It independently descends from their proximate common exemplar and supplied Steele's text where Lambeth f. 18 is mutilated. The codex also contains Walter Hilton's Mixed Life, a medical treatise, and a South English Legendary extract.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490
entities.description: The principal witness for Steele's James Yonge version. The target is the codex's second Middle English item, following an English Conquest of Ireland, and runs from f. 28v through f. 72r in Steele's milestone sequence. The current summary TEI assigns the parchment codex a 1400–1410 date that predates Yonge's securely dated 1422 translation; this unresolved conflict is not normalized away.

date_statements.label: current whole-codex catalog date
date_statements.notes: The catalog value conflicts with the 1422 translation date for item 2 and needs part-level review.

holding.notes: Formerly owned by Richard Rawlinson and bequeathed to the Bodleian in 1755.

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 633
entities.description: A complete late-fifteenth-century collateral copy of James Yonge's 1422 Secretum version in a parchment codex of 85 folios, ending f. 84r with a closing that differs from Steele's Rawlinson-based ending. Steele explicitly supplies printed p. 191 through p. 196 line 5 from Lambeth f. 46 line 21 while Rawlinson is defective. The XML's second L-marked folio sequence likely maps this witness but is not confirmed.

holding.notes: James describes the manuscript at printed pp. 780–781. No exact current CalmView record was found on 2026-07-15.

notes: 28 or varying lines to a page; late current hand; morocco binding with Carew shield inlaid.

entities.label: Lambeth LUNA selected image of MS 633 f. 84r
entities.description: The only public LUNA target-bearing image found for MS 633. Visual inspection confirms Yonge's terminal prose, a two-line English closing epigraph, and later pen trials on the same leaf.
entities.reproduction_format: single 1489 × 1908 JPEG exposed through IIIF Presentation 2 and Image 2

entities.label: Lambeth LUNA selected image of MS 633 f. 85v
entities.description: A public supporting image whose catalog title names James Yonge's Secretum but whose pictured surface contains later pen trials and no target-work text.
entities.reproduction_format: single 1489 × 1985 JPEG exposed through IIIF Presentation 2 and Image 2

entities.label: French F3 version of the Secretum secretorum
entities.description: The conceptual anonymous French source-version tradition called F3 in the source scholarship. This entity does not stand for one identified surviving manuscript or assert that any extant F3 witness was the physical exemplar used by an English translator.

entities.label: Latin vulgate version of the Secretum secretorum
entities.description: The conceptual Latin vulgate source tradition associated with Philip of Tripoli. It is modeled as one disputed alternative for the second English version, not as an identified physical exemplar.

entities.label: Jofroi of Waterford's French version of the Secretum secretorum
entities.description: The conceptual French source version on which Steele says the greater portion of James Yonge's English adaptation depends. This entity neither identifies Yonge's physical exemplar nor treats his Irish additions and adaptation as translated wholesale from Jofroi.

entities.label: British Library current catalog description of Royal MS 18 A VII
entities.description: The interim Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record used for current shelfmark, contents, folio boundary, source description, date, physical description, provenance, digitized-content field, and request route.

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library current CalmView record for MS 501
entities.description: Current holding-library catalog record, based on M. R. James, including the witness description and microfilm reference.

entities.label: M. R. James's 1932 catalog of Lambeth medieval manuscripts
entities.description: The detailed catalog source for MSS 501 and 633, publicly digitized by Heidelberg. Its MS 633 entry says the manuscript is not mentioned by Steele even though Steele's 1898 edition explicitly uses Lambeth 633 at p. 191; the contradiction is retained.

bibliographic.citation: Montague Rhodes James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of Lambeth Palace: The Mediaeval Manuscripts (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1932).
bibliographic.title: A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of Lambeth Palace: The Mediaeval Manuscripts

entities.label: Bodleian current catalog TEI for MS. Laud Misc. 685
entities.description: The current summary TEI from the official bodleian/medieval-mss repository, pinned at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496 and cached as exact XML.

entities.label: Bodleian current catalog TEI for MS. Rawl. B. 490
entities.description: The current summary TEI from the official bodleian/medieval-mss repository, pinned at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496 and cached as exact XML.

entities.label: Laing's 1978 linguistic study of Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685
entities.description: A two-volume Edinburgh thesis whose manuscript study on vol. 1 pp. 292–311 reconstructs the witness relationship and scribal-language layers. It is a supporting scholarly description, not a textual ancestor or holding-library catalog.

bibliographic.citation: Margaret Laing, Studies in the Dialect Material of Mediaeval Lincolnshire (PhD thesis, University of Edinburgh, 1978).
bibliographic.title: Studies in the Dialect Material of Mediaeval Lincolnshire
bibliographic.pages: two volumes; relevant study at vol. 1, pp. 292–311

entities.label: Manzalaoui, Secretum secretorum: Nine English Versions (1977)
entities.description: A later EETS edition and source study. Its full controlled-digital-lending text was not accessed; Zamuner explicitly reports its p. xlvii conclusion that the Lambeth 501/Laud 685 version descends directly from French F3 rather than Latin.

bibliographic.citation: Secretum secretorum: Nine English Versions, ed. M. A. Manzalaoui, vol. I, EETS Original Series 276 (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1977).
bibliographic.title: Secretum secretorum: Nine English Versions
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 276

entities.label: Steele, Secretum secretorum cum glossis et notulis (1920)
entities.description: Steele's later Latin Secretum edition and source census, consulted for his revised classification of English manuscript versions. It is supporting scholarship, not an ancestor of the 1898 edition or 1997 encoding.

bibliographic.citation: Pseudo-Aristotle, Secretum secretorum cum glossis et notulis, ed. Robert Steele, Opera hactenus inedita Rogeri Baconi, fasc. 5 (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1920).
bibliographic.title: Secretum secretorum cum glossis et notulis

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Steele's 1920 edition
entities.description: A complete 396-page PDF scan with OCR and metadata, cached as a supporting source.

agents.name: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative
alternate_names: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse

agents.name: Sencor
notes: Named as the initial keyboarding provider in the XML editorial declaration.

agents.name: T. Henderson
notes: Compiler of the glossary omitted from the U-M encoding.

scope.notes: The file header identifies U-M HTI and the persistent SSecr item; no evidence of an intervening independent textual revision was found.

object.description: Steele's Note and the three edited texts

scope.notes: Other print front matter and Henderson's glossary pp. 249–293 are outside the encoding.

subject.description: The first Middle English version, The Secrete of Secretes

object.description: the shortened French F3 source tradition underlying this English version

scope.notes: The relation identifies translation and abridgement from a conceptual French source version. It does not identify the translator's physical French exemplar.

subject.description: The Governance of Lordschipes as represented by Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685

scope.notes: Steele's Latin-vulgate derivation and Manzalaoui's French-F3 derivation are mutually incompatible scholarly alternatives. This tentative edge records Steele's position without choosing it or asserting an identifiable Latin exemplar.

subject.description: The Governance of Lordschipes as represented by Lambeth 501 and Laud Misc. 685

scope.notes: Manzalaoui's French-F3 derivation and Steele's Latin-vulgate derivation are mutually incompatible scholarly alternatives. This tentative edge records Manzalaoui's position as explicitly reported by Zamuner without choosing it or asserting an identifiable French exemplar.

subject.description: the substantial Jofroi-derived portion of James Yonge's third Middle English version

object.description: corresponding material in Jofroi of Waterford's French version

scope.notes: Steele says the greater portion is a direct translation. The relation is therefore passage-scoped: it does not derive Yonge's whole adaptation, Irish material, or interpolations from Jofroi, and it does not identify a physical French exemplar.

locators: pp. 41–118 except the Laud-supplied mutilated passage

subject.description: passage replacing the mutilated Lambeth f. 18

locators: parallel text corresponding to Lambeth f. 18r–v

scope.notes: Laud is also an independent complete sibling witness of the version, not merely a patch fragment.

assertion.notes: Laing reconstructs X from synchronized linguistic strata and shared errors; X itself is not extant.
Disjunctive relict forms make direct copying from Lambeth 501 unlikely.

subject.description: third edited version except Lambeth-supplied lacuna

locators: Rawlinson resumes at f. 55 after the lacuna

scope.notes: Lambeth 633 is a complete collateral copy ending f. 84r; the relation does not reduce it to the supplied passage.

rights.component: The medieval Middle English texts and their underlying pseudo-Aristotelian work traditions
rights.basis: The medieval works and their known translators predate modern copyright by centuries; the texts were also published in Steele's 1898 edition, and the U.S. Copyright Office states that all works published before 1931 are now public domain in the United States.

rights.component: Pinned 1997 electronic encoding and embedded availability statement
rights.basis: The XML permits personal, research, and teaching copies when its statement is included, permits linking, and expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or non-profit content providers; it does not state a copyright owner or open license.
notes: These legacy item terms are not erased by the CME repository's software Unlicense or by rights in the medieval text and 1898 edition.

rights.component: Current U-M CME raw-file distribution and digital presentation
rights.basis: U-M's current bulk-download page says CME raw files are available without restrictions on use or reuse, but the pinned SSecr file carries a stricter legacy item statement. The conflict prevents a broader copyright-status conclusion for this exact encoding.

rights.component: Steele's 1898 edition, editorial text, and Henderson glossary
rights.basis: The volume was first published in 1898; the U.S. Copyright Office states that all works published before 1931 are now public domain in the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan, OCR, metadata, and scan-data carriers
rights.basis: The scan represents a public-domain 1898 volume and is publicly downloadable, but the IA item metadata supplies no explicit rights or license field for the digital carriers.

rights.component: Medieval manuscript material and target text
rights.basis: The fifteenth-century material and text are centuries outside the U.S. copyright term.

rights.component: Any future British Library-supplied photographs or reproductions
rights.basis: No images were obtained. British Library Digitisation Services requires users to secure applicable permissions and supplies images under its service terms.

rights.component: British Library catalog-page text
rights.basis: The catalog page states that British Library text is © British Library Board and available under CC BY 4.0 except where otherwise stated.

rights.component: Laing's 1978 thesis text, layout, and repository derivatives
rights.basis: The repository publicly distributes the thesis and extracted text but its item metadata exposes no open reuse license. Public access is not treated as public-domain or open-license status.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete 1898 volume with title matter, Steele's short Note, three texts, glossary, and other bound matter
editorial_practices.representation: Steele's Note and the three prose texts through printed p. 248
editorial_practices.description: The U-M encoding includes the editorial Note and all three version texts but omits other front matter and Henderson's glossary pp. 249–293.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Steele's editorial notes and textual glosses
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial notes retained; textual glosses excluded
editorial_practices.description: The electronic editorial declaration explicitly distinguishes included editorial notes from omitted textual glosses.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Printed page breaks, manuscript folio markers, and two milestone sequences in the third version
editorial_practices.representation: All relevant markers retained, including the unconfirmed L-marked second sequence
editorial_practices.description: The encoding retains the third version's dual manuscript milestone sets rather than silently selecting one, and records its uncertainty in the editorial declaration.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Damage and lacunae in the principal Lambeth 501 and Rawlinson B. 490 witnesses
editorial_practices.representation: Corresponding text supplied from Laud Misc. 685 and Lambeth 633
editorial_practices.description: Steele supplies the second version's mutilated Lambeth f. 18 from Laud and the third version's p. 191–196 lacuna from Lambeth 633 before Rawlinson resumes at f. 55.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript text without all modern chapter and page aids
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial headings, chapter numbering, brackets, and third-version contents-page numbers
editorial_practices.description: Steele/U-M distinguish editorial navigation from manuscript content; the XML notes that page numbers were added to the third version's contents for readers.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/SSecr.xml.
evidence.locator: HEADER; outer FRONT; three grouped TEXT children
evidence.summary: Identifies SSecr, U-M HTI, 1997 date, Steele 1898 source edition, exact electronic editorial practices and legacy availability terms, three version boundaries and titles, witness milestones, omissions, supply notes, Yonge dedication, and encoded text.

evidence.citation: Structural and fixity inspection of the pinned SSecr XML at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Verified 628,610 bytes, git blob b8cfa252194419fb04200d8f46eb67fa1f8737b1, SHA-256, three grouped TEXT streams, 148 DIV1, 183 DIV2, 8 DIV3, 359 HEAD, 354 P, 9 L, 248 PB, and 379 MILESTONE elements; bounded print and manuscript locators without treating encoding-node counts as authored units.

evidence.citation: Kyvero Press CME repository, LICENSE (Unlicense).
evidence.summary: The CME repository describes its software as dedicated to the public domain under the Unlicense. This is retained as repository-layer evidence and not generalized to editions, manuscript images, or provider-supplied content.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse and CME raw-file download statement.
evidence.summary: Current public collection context, public-domain source-edition keying history, raw XML bulk-download route, and a broad current statement that CME raw files are available without restrictions on use or reuse.
notes: The broad current reuse statement is recorded alongside, not in place of, SSecr's stricter embedded legacy availability paragraph.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library CalmView, MSS/501, Secreta Secretorum.
evidence.summary: Current holding record based on James, including shelfmark, late-fifteenth-century description, f. 18 mutilation, f. 42r ending, and Film 1228 microfilm reference.

evidence.citation: Montague Rhodes James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the Manuscripts in the Library of Lambeth Palace: The Mediaeval Manuscripts (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1932).
evidence.locator: MS 501, printed pp. 703–704; MS 633, printed pp. 780–781
evidence.summary: Physical descriptions, contents, exact ends, f. 18 damage, Yonge dedication and variant ending, former letter designations, and edition references for both Lambeth witnesses.

evidence.citation: Heidelberg University Library IIIF manifest for James 1932.
evidence.summary: The complete digital object exposes Creative Commons Public Domain Mark 1.0 as its rights statement.

evidence.citation: World Microfilms, Lambeth Palace Library: The Medieval Manuscripts.
evidence.locator: Section I list: MS 501 on reel 11; MS 633 on reel 15
evidence.summary: States that the medieval-manuscript collection is available in its entirety on 172 positive-roll microfilms and maps MS 501 and MS 633 to exact reels.

evidence.citation: Lambeth LUNA exact public API search for MS501*.
evidence.summary: Dated exact search returned totalResults 0; no public LUNA image object for MS 501 was found.

evidence.citation: Lambeth LUNA exact public API search for MS633*.
evidence.summary: Dated exact search returned exactly two Image records, MS633f84r and MS633f85v, both late-fifteenth-century vellum with Rights: LPL and separate IIIF manifests; no complete codex object was returned.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of Lambeth Palace Library MS 633 f. 84r native IIIF image.
evidence.summary: Confirms the target work's final prose and two-line closing epigraph on f. 84r, followed by later pen trials. The bundle captures the complete one-image selected object at native width.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of Lambeth Palace Library MS 633 f. 85v native IIIF image.
evidence.summary: Confirms that f. 85v contains later pen trials and no target-work text, despite the image record's broad James Yonge/Secretum title.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library, Application for Permission to Reproduce Images.
evidence.summary: Current reproduction route and terms: permission is non-exclusive, copyright may restrict use, Lambeth does not own copyright in all holdings, and rights granted are limited to specified uses.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Laud Misc. 685, TEI record at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark and ARKs, Middle English Secretum first item, other codex contents, parchment support, fifteenth-century date, source-catalog provenance, and absence of a digital surrogate element.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Rawl. B. 490, TEI record at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark and ARKs, Conquest of Ireland and James Young Secretum items, Middle English language, parchment support, 1400–1410 whole-codex date, Rawlinson provenance, and absence of a digital surrogate element.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian JSON-LD search API checks for Rawl. B. 490, Rawlinson B. 490, and Laud Misc. 685.
evidence.summary: Requests with Accept: application/ld+json returned totalItems 0 for each exact query on 2026-07-15. Combined with the current catalog TEIs' absent surrogate elements, no complete public Digital Bodleian object was verified.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special Collections and ordering guidance.
evidence.summary: Current physical special-collections consultation and enquiry route.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special collections mediated copying.
evidence.summary: Reference-quality scan ordering for manuscripts, exact shelfmark/folio requirements, current fee guidance, conservation exceptions, and Imaging Services terms for supplied images.

evidence.citation: Margaret Laing, Studies in the Dialect Material of Mediaeval Lincolnshire (PhD thesis, University of Edinburgh, 1978), vol. 1, pp. 292–311.
evidence.summary: Shared omissions, errors, and synchronized dialect strata establish a proximate common exemplar for Lambeth 501 and Laud 685; disjunctive variants exclude direct copying of one extant witness from the other. It also supplies target folios and southwest-Lincolnshire scribal localization with southern and West-Midland layers.

evidence.citation: Edinburgh Research Archive metadata for Margaret Laing's 1978 thesis, handle 1842/7336.
evidence.summary: Confirms author, title, thesis type, institution, 1978 date, two public source PDFs, repository-generated extracted text, and absence of an exposed open-license field in the item metadata.

evidence.citation: Ilaria Zamuner, ‘La tradizione romanza del Secretum secretorum pseudo-aristotelico: Regesto delle versioni e dei manoscritti,’ Studi Medievali 46.1 (2005): 31–116.
evidence.summary: Explicitly reports Manzalaoui's p. xlvii view that the Lambeth 501/Laud 685 English version descends directly from French F3 rather than the Latin text.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for secretumsecretor0001unse, Manzalaoui's 1977 EETS edition.
evidence.summary: Confirms bibliographic identity, EETS Original Series 276, Oxford University Press, 1977, and controlled-digital-lending access. The unencrypted PDF and OCR derivatives are marked private; they were not accessed or cached.

evidence.citation: Pseudo-Aristotle, Secretum secretorum cum glossis et notulis, ed. Robert Steele, Opera hactenus inedita Rogeri Baconi, fasc. 5 (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1920).
evidence.summary: Steele's later census classifies Royal as French-F3-derived, Lambeth 501/Laud 685 as Latin-vulgate-derived, and Yonge's Rawlinson/Lambeth tradition as Jofroi-French-derived in 1422. Its 'Lamb. Carew 596' line conflicts with the actual contents of Carew 596 and is not substituted for MS 633.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for operahactenusine05baco.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public scan identity and complete downloadable PDF/OCR derivatives for Steele's 1920 volume.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, What Is Copyright? and Duration of Copyright (Circular 15A), current 2026 guidance.
evidence.summary: Current official term guidance, including the statement that all works published in the United States before 1 January 1931 are in the public domain.

open_questions.question: Does the Lambeth 501/Laud 685 English version descend directly from the Latin vulgate, as Steele states, or from Anglo-Norman French F3, as Manzalaoui argues?
next_steps: Consult Manzalaoui's full 1977 introduction at p. xlvii through legitimate borrowing or library access.
Compare shared innovations in both English witnesses against representative Latin-vulgate and F3 witnesses before asserting encoded_from or transcribes relations to a source-language witness.

open_questions.question: Does the XML's L-marked folio sequence, approximately 3r–84r, represent Lambeth MS 633 throughout?
next_steps: Compare every L-marked milestone against the complete MS 633 microfilm on reel 15 or the physical manuscript.
Request U-M production documentation for the dual milestone encoding.

open_questions.question: Can complete reusable digital facsimiles of Royal 18 A VII, Lambeth 501, Laud Misc. 685, Rawl. B. 490, or Lambeth 633 be obtained?
next_steps: Recheck British Library digitized content after cyber-attack recovery.
Ask Lambeth for complete microfilm-derived copies of MSS 501 and 633 and clarify reuse terms.
Use Bodleian mediated copying for Laud ff. 1r–57v and Rawlinson ff. 28v–72r, or request whole-codex scans if conservation and budget allow.
Do not label the two selected MS 633 LUNA images as a complete manuscript facsimile.

open_questions.question: Why does the current Bodleian summary TEI date Rawl. B. 490 to 1400–1410 when its second item is James Yonge's securely dated 1422 translation?
next_steps: Consult the full Rawlinson Quarto Catalogue entry and physical codicology for part-level dating.
Ask Bodleian whether the summary date is a legacy whole-volume value requiring correction or qualification.

open_questions.question: What is Lambeth MS 633's exact current CalmView record, microfilm identifier, and current request status?
next_steps: Ask Lambeth Palace Library to provide the current catalog URI and preservation-microfilm reference for MS 633.
Retain James's detailed entry and the exact LUNA records until a current holding record is supplied.

open_questions.question: What produced Steele's 1920 census reading 'Lamb. Carew 596' for the Yonge tradition, when Lambeth Carew 596 contains the Song of Dermot and the Earl and James catalogs Yonge's Secretum as MS 633?
next_steps: Inspect corrected copies, errata, and Steele's working references for a typographical or OCR-independent explanation.
Continue to identify the Yonge collateral witness as MS 633 based on Steele's 1898 supply note, James, LUNA, and World Microfilms.

open_questions.question: What exact production files and transformations connect Steele's print volume, the initial Sencor keyboarding, the complete Borger/Church correction, U-M's current presentation, and the pinned CME XML?
next_steps: Request U-M HTI production history, raw-file version dates, and validation notes for SSecr.
Compare a current raw U-M download byte-for-byte and structurally with the pinned XML when an exact direct item download is exposed.
